name: Highlining
description: Highlining is walking a one-inch-wide piece of nylon webbing tensioned between anchor points at extreme heights, often hundreds of meters above the ground. Unlike tightropes, slacklines stretch and sway, requiring continuous micro-corrections. The longest highline walked is 2,710 meters in France (2022), and the ISA has recorded 11 fatalities since 1983, nearly all from rigging errors or forgetting to clip a safety leash.

thumbnail.alt: Highliner walking a slackline stretched between mountain cliffs above a deep valley

media.title: Friedi Kuhne walks the world's longest free solo highline

time_to_competence.notes: Walking a short slackline near the ground takes days to weeks. Transitioning to height introduces crippling fear that resets perceived ability.

time_to_expert.notes: Walking long highlines (500m+) in exposed conditions requires years of progressive exposure. Free solo highlining without a leash is the pinnacle, achieved by very few.

feedback_delay.notes: You either stay on the line or you fall off. Balance corrections are instantaneous and continuous.

failure_cost.notes: With a leash, falls are dramatic but survivable. Without a leash (free solo), a fall is fatal. Rigging failures can be catastrophic. 11 deaths since 1983.

cognitive_demand.notes: Requires intense focus and fear management. The cognitive challenge is suppressing the survival instinct while maintaining calm micro-corrections.

physical_demand.notes: Demands extraordinary core strength, proprioception, and leg endurance. Wind resistance and line sway add constant physical load.

prerequisite_knowledge.notes: Must understand rigging systems, redundancy principles, anchor integrity, and webbing physics before attempting height. Errors in setup are lethal.

measurability.notes: Distance walked, height, and whether the line was completed without falls are all precisely measurable. ISA verifies records.

perishability.notes: Balance and fear management degrade without practice. The psychological component is especially perishable -- extended breaks amplify exposure anxiety.

precision_required.notes: Foot placement on a 1-inch line requires extreme precision. Weight distribution and center of gravity must be continuously managed.

ambiguity.notes: Did you walk the line or not? Distance records are precisely measured. Some subjectivity in style for non-record contexts.

instruction_dependency.notes: Rigging knowledge is safety-critical and must be learned from experienced practitioners. Balance technique can be self-taught but progression is much safer with mentorship.

environmental_variability.notes: Wind, temperature, humidity, line tension, altitude, and terrain all affect difficulty dramatically. No two highlines are alike.

concurrent_skills.notes: Simultaneously managing balance, fear response, breathing, line reading, and wind compensation. At height, cognitive load multiplies.

plateau_density.notes: Fear barriers create distinct plateaus. Moving from 50m to 100m height, or from leashed to free solo, each represent psychological walls.

domain_evolution.notes: Rapidly evolving since the 1980s. Webbing technology, rigging standards, and record distances continue advancing. ISA formed in 2015.

observability.notes: Among the most visually dramatic human activities. A person on a thin line between cliffs is instantly comprehensible and awe-inspiring.

luck_variance.notes: Sudden wind gusts introduce unpredictability. Equipment quality and anchor conditions vary. But skilled highliners manage controllable risk.

social_coordination.notes: Walking is individual, but rigging requires a team. Safety partners and belayers are essential for responsible practice.

methodology_notes: Death and safety statistics from the International Slackline Association. World records from ISA-verified records database. Equipment and rigging standards from ISA risk assessments.
